name: Western Union Money Order Only
description_short: Western Union is a multinational financial services corporation founded in 1851, now focused on money transfers, wire transfers, and money orders for domestic and international customers.
description_long:
Western Union was founded on April 8, 1851, as the New York and Mississippi Valley Printing Telegraph Company in Rochester, New York. The company dominated American telegraphy for over a century, pioneering telegraph technology and related services. In 1856, it merged with competitors and adopted the Western Union Telegraph Company name. The company successfully completed the first transcontinental telegraph in 1861, with initial messages sent to President Abraham Lincoln. By the mid-1800s, Western Union had transformed into a national oligopolist controlling most of the American telegraphy industry.

After experiencing financial difficulties in the 1980s, Western Union strategically shifted away from its core communications business and increasingly focused on financial services. The company launched wire money transfer services in 1871 and developed additional financial offerings including money orders, bill payment, and transfer tracking services. By 2006, Western Union ceased all communications operations and had fully transitioned to being a financial services company. The New York Times noted at that time that Western Union was "the world's largest money-transfer business," a position strengthened by the large immigrant population regularly wiring money home.

Today, Western Union operates as a public company traded on the NYSE under ticker WU and is a component of the S&P 600. Headquartered in Denver, Colorado, the company serves customers worldwide through its extensive network. With 9,100 employees as of December 31, 2024, Western Union generated $4.36 billion in revenue in 2023, with operating income of $817.5 million and net income of $626 million. The company maintains total assets of $8.199 billion.

Western Union's transition from communications pioneer to financial services provider represents a significant business evolution. While the company achieved dominant market position in money transfers, it operates in a competitive landscape that has expanded with digital payment platforms and other transfer services. The company's longevity—173+ years in operation—demonstrates institutional stability, though modern fintech competitors continue to reshape the money transfer industry.
pros: Established global reach with 174+ years of operational history and worldwide service coverage
Comprehensive financial service offerings including wire transfers, money orders, money transfers, bill pay, transfer tracking, and price estimation
Public company with substantial financial resources: $4.36 billion in 2023 revenue and $8.199 billion in total assets
Historically recognized as the world's largest money-transfer business with strong brand recognition
Large employee base (9,100 employees) providing operational support and network infrastructure
Successful business model adaptation—transitioned from telegraphy to money services and remains profitable
NYSE-traded company (WU) with transparent financial reporting and S&P 600 component status
cons: Limited information on fee structures, exchange rates, or pricing transparency from provided Wikipedia source
Faces increasing competition from digital fintech platforms and newer money transfer services not mentioned in profile
Ceased communications operations entirely by 2006, representing loss of formerly core business line
No specific data provided on customer service quality, complaint resolution, or regulatory compliance record
Profile does not address international regulatory challenges or compliance costs in global money transfer operations
best_for: Immigrants and expatriates sending money internationally to family and home countries
Customers requiring traditional money orders for payments and financial transactions
Individuals needing wire transfer services with established, reputable financial institutions
Users seeking bill payment services through an established, long-standing financial services provider
diagnosis: Western Union is best for customers seeking traditional, established money transfer and money order services, particularly immigrants sending remittances internationally. The primary caveat is that Western Union operates in an increasingly competitive landscape with modern digital alternatives, and specific information about current fees, exchange rates, and service comparisons is not available in the provided source material.
